mnemonics.title: Bayes Tea Party
mnemonics.content: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B) — Remember 'BAYEs Tea Party': B (信念/Belief) from A (先验/Prior) adjusted by evidence — Think of updating your belief about what tea you want after seeing what tea is available.

mnemonics.title: Naive Nancy
mnemonics.content: Naive = Nancy assumes features don't know each other. Nancy is 'naive' thinking each feature works independently, even when they're actually correlated in real life.

mnemonics.title: MAP Magic Formula
mnemonics.content: MAP = Maximize P(Class|Features) = P(Features|Class) × P(Class). Think 'Most Awesome Prediction' — choose the class that gives the highest posterior probability.

mnemonics.title: Smoothing Shield
mnemonics.content: Laplace Smoothing adds +1 to every count: (count + 1) / (total + vocabulary). Imagine every word gets a 'shield' (+1) so no probability becomes zero — like giving every player a starting point in a game.

acronyms.usage: Choose the class with highest posterior probability: argmax P(Class|Features)
Estimating parameters by maximizing P(Features|Class) — finding the model that makes observed data most probable.
Use when features follow normal/gaussian distribution — continuous data like height, weight, temperature.
Use for document classification with word counts — text mining, spam detection.
Use for binary/boolean features — word presence/absence in documents.
Use for count data following Poisson distribution — events occurring in fixed intervals.
Add 1 to numerator, vocabulary size to denominator: prevents zero probabilities for unseen features.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a flow chart: Prior P(A) → Evidence P(B) → Likelihood P(B|A) → Posterior P(A|B). Draw arrows showing how prior belief gets updated by new evidence to produce posterior.
Draw circles (features) that don't touch each other — no overlapping areas. Even though in reality they might overlap, Naive Bayes pretends each circle floats independently.
Think of a probability scale (0 to 1). Start with Prior at some point, slide along the scale based on Likelihood, end at Posterior — the final position determines your prediction.
For Gaussian NB, draw bell curves for each class. For Multinomial, draw bar charts of word frequencies. For Bernoulli, draw binary checkmarks (✓/✗) for each feature.
Imagine two overlapping colored fog areas (classes). For a new point, find which fog color is 'denser' at that location — that's your predicted class.

analogies.analogy: Medical Diagnosis — Before any test (prior), you have a base chance of having a disease. The test results (evidence/likelihood) update that chance to give your actual diagnosis (posterior). Like a doctor revising your diagnosis based on new test reports.

Job Interview — A naive interviewer assumes each interview question scores you independently, ignoring that good answers to one question might predict good answers to others. In reality, skills are correlated, but we 'naively' treat them as separate.

Restaurant Menu — If you've never tried a dish (zero count), you don't say it's impossible! Instead, assume at least one person ordered it (+1 smoothing). This prevents ruling out new, unseen options completely.

Email Sorting — Like a secretary who learned from past letters. If many spam emails had words like 'WIN', 'FREE', 'PRIZE', any new email with these words gets flagged as spam based on learned patterns.
Weather Forecast — Before looking at today's data (prior), meteorologists know the season's average: '30% chance of rain in summer'. This base rate shapes the final prediction.

analogies.analogram: DNA Matching — Like finding a criminal by checking if DNA features match the crime scene. P(Evidence| Criminal) is high if features align, low if they don't — similar to likelihood in Naive Bayes.
